macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna on a Besieged City and the Status of Kohanic Wives

micro_segments.title: Overview of Ketubot 27 and the Context of Women's Personal Status
The Mishna: A City Under Siege (Ir Shekvasha Karkom)
Kohanic Wives Found in the City Are Assumed Raped and Forbidden
Exception: Witnesses That Women Were Not Raped Can Testify

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Challenge: The Wine Question and Distinguishing Between Siege and Passage

micro_segments.title: The Contradiction: Wine Concern in Peacetime vs. Wartime; Sex Concern in Siege
First Answer: Sex is Always a Priority, Wine is Not
Second Answer: Distinction Between One's Own Country and Foreign Enemy Territory
Tosafot's Reading: Time Constraint in Enemy Territory vs. Security at Home

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Narrowing: Even in a Besieged City, Escape Routes and Hiding Places Create Doubt

micro_segments.title: The Assumption That All Women Were Raped Is Questioned
Response: Guards and Patrols Ensure No Escape and Systematic Violation
Counter-Response: Even Guards Sleep; How Can We Assume Total Prevention?
Rabbi Levi's Answer: Chains, Dogs, and Geese Create an Alarm System
Debate of Tannaim: One Distinguishes Countries; Other Demands Physical Proof

macro_segments.title: If One Hiding Place or Tunnel Exists, All Women Are Permitted—The Principle of Possible Escape

micro_segments.title: Rav Idi bar Avin: Even One Tunnel Permits All Kohanic Wives
Rabbi Yirmiya's Question: Does One Hiding Place for One Person Permit Everyone?
Comparison to Two Paths (Tamei/Tahor): Can We Give Lenient Rulings to Multiple People?
Rava's Resolution: Coming Separately vs. Together Changes the Ruling
Application: Unlike the Two Paths Case, Here We Have Only Suspicion, Not Certainty

macro_segments.title: A Woman's Statement That She Was Not Raped Is Believed via Migo (She Could Have Said Otherwise)

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Question: Can a Woman Claim She Wasn't Raped Without Claiming She Escaped?
Comparison to the Rented Donkey Case: Migo (She Could Have Lied) and Testimony
Abaye's Limitation: Migo Does Not Apply Against Public/General Knowledge
Application to Captive Women: Only Suspicion Exists, Not Certain Fact; Migo Applies

macro_segments.title: Credibility of Witnesses in Captivity Cases: Slaves, Servants, and Testimony Standards

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: Even Slaves and Slave Women Are Believed as Witnesses
Contradiction: Another Baraita Says Slave Women (Especially Her Own) Are Not Believed

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Get and the Danger of Seclusion: Why Slave Women's Testimony Cannot Prevent Seclusion

micro_segments.title: Legal Issue: After a Get, Seclusion Between Ex-Spouses Is Forbidden
Witnesses Needed: A Slave Woman's Testimony of Her Presence Proves No Seclusion
Except for Her Own Slave: Not Believed Because Women Act Freely Before Slaves

macro_segments.title: Credibility of Witnesses in Captivity Cases: Slaves, Servants, and Testimony Standards (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rav Pappa's Distinction: Her Own Slave Woman vs. Husband's Slave Woman
The Principle: We Believe Testimony; We Don't Believe Active Lying
Rav Ashi's Refinement: Silent Withholding vs. Active Testimony; Context Matters

macro_segments.title: Slaves and Intimidation: The Mari Bar Isak Case and the Limits of Coercion

micro_segments.title: The Story: A Brother Claims Inheritance; Witnesses Refuse to Testify Out of Fear
Rav Chisda's Unusual Ruling: Fear of Violence Shifts the Burden of Proof
The Principle: People Withhold Information Under Fear But Don't Testify Falsely

macro_segments.title: Expanding Credible Witnesses: Children, Parents, Siblings Can Testify About Non-Rape

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Men, Women, Children—All Can Testify a Woman Was Not Raped
Exception: Her Own Son and Daughter Cannot Testify
Another Baraita: Everyone Can Testify Except the Woman Herself and Her Husband
Reconciliation: Rav Pappa and Rav Ashi Represent Different Tannaitic Views

macro_segments.title: Innocent Storytelling (Masiach Lefi Tumo): When Testimony Is Believed Without Formal Intent

micro_segments.title: Definition and Application: A Person Speaking Innocently Without Legal Intent
The Case of the Woman and Her Mother in Captivity: Son's Protective Vigil
Rabbi's Ruling: Mother Permitted to a Kohen Based on Son's Innocent Account

macro_segments.title: Conclusion: The Mishna Is Nearly Moot—Multiple Leniencies Prevent Actual Forbiddance

micro_segments.title: Summary of Leniencies: Escape Routes, Hiding Places, Migo, Masiach Lefi Tumo
Practical Result: Almost No Actual Case Will Result in Forbiddance

macro_segments.title: The Husband's Testimony: A Kohanic Husband Cannot Testify That His Wife Was Not Raped

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Zechariah ben HaKatzav: Historical Account of Ensuring Non-Rape During Siege
Gemara's Response: A Man Cannot Testify About Himself (or by Extension, His Wife)
Legal Consequence: Even a Husband's Constant Presence Cannot Permit His Wife

macro_segments.title: Protective Arrangement: A Kohanic Husband's Legal Workaround to Maintain Marriage

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Separate Housing in the Same Compound to Avoid Seclusion
Practical Measures: Wife Leads Children Out; Follows Children In to Prevent Seclusion
Result: Marriage Maintained Legally While Ensuring No Opportunity for Invalidation

topical_tags.term: Distinction: own country vs. foreign territory
People withhold information but don't lie (tartei lo avdi)
Self-testimony forbidden (ein adam meid al atzmo)
